Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative proverb clause; elder noun phrase as subject + negative stative predicate + locative complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Advice from elders may be ignored at first, but it remains valuable and often becomes useful later.
Usage Contexts: family advice; youth correction; dispute mediation; village meetings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Elder-council speech associated with Guiglo and Duékoué family mediation; foregrounds age, memory, and communal responsibility.
First Example Sentence: Mɔ nɛ ao -saaɛ, zɩ 'bhle se -nɩ 'kpɔn tɩ, 'wlu zɩ a -tɩ.
Second Example Sentence: Kɔfi dɩ sɔɔ, ka zɩ 'bhle se -nɩ 'kpɔn tɩ 'nynɩ ɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Ɛ -wɛe gbla -dho, zɩ 'bhle se -nɩ 'kpɔn tɩ, ao 'de dɩ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional proverb; temporal subordinate clause + negative main clause with inanimate subject.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When danger is evident, a wise person stops arguing and takes action.
Usage Contexts: travel warnings; political advice; farming decisions; conflict avoidance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: River-crossing imagery linked to forest routes and market movement around western Ivorian waterways.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [ɲí wlù kɔ́ː piɾoɡɩ̀ sè dʰò tɩ̀]
First Example Sentence: Nyi 'wlu -kɔɔ, pirogɩ se 'dho -tɩ, -bo ke gbla nɛ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphorical animal proverb; locative noun phrase + negative reflexive-like predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person’s real character cannot stay hidden forever.
Usage Contexts: character judgment; leadership critique; family talk; storytelling
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Forest-animal imagery drawing on leopard symbolism in western forest communities.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [ɡɔ́ː klɔ́ː ʊ̀n ɛ̀ sè ɡ͡bɔ́n nɩ̀]
First Example Sentence: Gɔɔ 'klɔɔ 'ʋn, ɛ se 'gbɔn 'nɩ, yɛɛ dɩ nɛ a 'sia.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compact fixed clause; nominalized body-part subject + negative predicate + path noun complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A steady and patient person does not lose the way to a goal.
Usage Contexts: work ethic; apprenticeship; farming; school encouragement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Path imagery associated with long forest walks, apprenticeship, and persistence in rural labor.

Idiom (Guere): Kɔ 'bɔɔ nɛ a 'nynɩ, -wɛe a 'wlu se nɩ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Causal metaphor clause; possessed heart noun phrase + evidential consequence clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Resentment inside someone eventually appears in their speech.
Usage Contexts: marriage disputes; negotiation; family reconciliation; workplace tension
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on heart-and-speech imagery common in counselling, apology, and reconciliation settings.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [kɔ́ bɔ́ː nɛ̀ á ɲɩ́nɩ̀ wɛ́ː á wlù sè nɩ̀]
First Example Sentence: Kɔ 'bɔɔ nɛ a 'nynɩ, -wɛe a 'wlu se nɩ, nɛɛ -tɩ.
Second Example Sentence: Ɛ 'wlu -gbo kpɛ, kɔ 'bɔɔ nɛ a 'nynɩ, -wɛe a 'wlu se nɩ.
Third Example Sentence: Ao gbla dɩ; kɔ 'bɔɔ nɛ a 'nynɩ, -wɛe a 'wlu se nɩ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Temporal proverb; time noun phrase + negated evaluative predicate with object noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: In difficult times, people should not despise small sources of help.
Usage Contexts: poverty; crisis support; mutual aid; humility
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Night-fire imagery related to household fires, scarcity, and shared survival in village compounds.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [dʒɾú dɔ́è ɡ͡bò ɡbɛ́ː sè kpɔ́n kɛ̀]

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Parallel proverb; repeated locative verb frame with two coordinated subjects.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Alcohol, excitement, or celebration can loosen people’s speech and reveal secrets.
Usage Contexts: celebrations; humor; caution before public speech; social gatherings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Palm-wine and public-palaver imagery associated with festive gatherings and market-day talk.
Third Example Sentence: Ɛ 'wlu kɛ, ka zɩ 'nynɩ taa 'de kɛɛ, 'bhle 'de -tɩ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical comparison clause; body-part noun subject + contrasted negative predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Fairness requires sharing benefits, not letting one side enjoy everything.
Usage Contexts: family sharing; market fairness; community work; resource distribution
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Eye-and-mouth imagery used to criticize unequal sharing in household and cooperative work.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [mɛ́ ɲɩ́ blè ɡ͡blà sè blè nɩ̀]
Second Example Sentence: Ao -nyɩ kaka kpɩ, mɛ 'nyɩ -ble, 'gbla se -ble nɩ.
Third Example Sentence: Mɛ 'nyɩ -ble, 'gbla se -ble nɩ, zɩ dɩ kpa wɔn.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Agentive proverb; relative clause with hand noun subject + negative result predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Anyone involved in a messy action leaves traces and shares responsibility.
Usage Contexts: accountability; legal mediation; politics; moral instruction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Palm-nut and farm-labor imagery linked to visible evidence after communal work or wrongdoing.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [k͡pá dɩ́ nɛ̀ jɔ̀ bò sèa nɩ̀]

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Antithetical proverb; parallel noun phrases with contrastive negative predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Paths may become overgrown, but spoken words keep travelling and cannot be fully buried.
Usage Contexts: rumor; public reputation; diplomacy; literature; warning against careless speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Road-and-mouth imagery reflecting oral transmission, reputation, and the authority of spoken testimony.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Guere): [sáː dián kèa ɡ͡blà dián sè kèa]
